coredata.dc:title: Graphene Quantum Dots enable digital communication through biological fluids
coredata.dc:creator: Fichera, Luca
Li-Destri, Giovanni
Tuccitto, Nunzio
coredata.dc:description: A method of communication through biological fluids is herein presented. This bioinspired approach does not use electromagnetic waves but rather the exchange of chemical systems – a method known as Molecular Communication (MC). In the example herein outlined, communication is achieved by exploiting the fluorescence properties of quantum dots which are then analysed in the domain of the Fourier transform. The problem is studied from a theoretical point of view by numerically solving the differential equation that governs the phenomenon. The theoretical results are exploited to develop a prototype MC platform that enables the communication of infection-induced temperature variations via biological fluids to a receiver that has the task of releasing an antibiotic drug.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Nanoparticles
Molecular communication
Infection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic concept for in-vivo Molecular Communication. A micro-robot monitors the temperature of a tissue using a specific sensor. It communicates temperature level encoding information inside fluorescent information nanoparticles and releasing them in the blood flow. Another micro-robot detects the information nanoparticles using a fluorescent detector and decodes the information. It could then release medication if necessary. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
A schematic representation of the prototypal molecular communication system.

(a) The probability distribution obtained solving the Advection Diffusion Equation using 200 mm path length, 0.35 mm radius, 0.4 mm/s flow velocity, 1.2 mm2/s diffusion coefficient and 5 mm initial pulse width. (b) Simulated fluorescent spectra approximated to a Gaussian with μ = 437 and σ = 47. (c) 3D plot of the signal reached by the receiver if it is able to acquire the entire spectra for each dt interval. The red line traced on the surface represents the back and forth explored by the receiver if it can scan the wavelengths according to a cos(ωt) function. On the plane P(t) vs Time is shown the projection of the red line obtained by several scans representing the signal that the receiver detects by scanning. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Signals obtained from simultaneosly releasing of two information nanoparticles having distinct fluorescence spectra. (b) Fourier transformations of signals in (a).(c) Signals detected by the receiver when the same fluorescent information particle is released at different times. (d) Fourier transformations of signals in (c). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Experimental Fluorescent spectra of the three commercial GQDs at 290 nm excitation wavelenght. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Detected signals of GQDs by scanning receiver. From sx: B-GQDs, C_G-GQDs mixture, G-GQDs and B_C_G-GQDs. (b) Fourier transformation of the signals in (a) and respective binary encoding meaning according to Equation (3). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Learning-plot obtained by PCA from the 35 simulated signals. We observe 7 dense clusters consisting of 5 points each. The inset represents a zoom near the cluster of B_C-GQDs. “Reference” is the reference signal point compared to the other four that are out of phase of a ΔT written in the figure itself. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Experimental signals detected by scanning receveicer. From Top to bottom: B-GQDs, C-GQDs, G-GQDs, B_G-GQDs mix, B_C_G-GQDs mix and respective binary encoding meaning according to equation (3). (b) Fit plot of experimental signals. In blue the points relative to the learning-plot previously obtained by the simulated signals. In orange the points relative to the experimental signals fitted on the learning dataset in Fig. 6.

(a) Transfer of the value + 0.8, in reference to a temperature variation, using ASCII language in binary coding. (b) Transfer of the value + 0.8, using a specific encoding method described in Table 1. It should be noted that the signals labeled with "checking" are not useful for the transfer of information but are used for a check of the state of operation of the communication. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Symbols encoding table for HMEC-1 cell monitoring.

head.ce:title: Graphene Quantum Dots enable digital communication through biological fluids

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Sciences, Università Degli Studi di Catania, Viale A. Doria 6, 95125 Catania, Italy
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Sciences, Universita Ddegli Studi di Catania, Viale A. Doria 6, 95125 Catania, Italy.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory for Molecular Surfaces and Nanotechnology-CSGI, Viale A. Doria 6, 95125 Catania, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory for Molecular Surfaces and Nanotechnology-CSGI

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory for Molecular Surfaces and Nanotechnology-CSGI, Viale A. Doria 6, 95125 Catania, Italy

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemical Sciences, Università Degli Studi di Catania, Viale A. Doria 6, 95125 Catania, Italy.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A method of communication through biological fluids is herein presented. This bioinspired approach does not use electromagnetic waves but rather the exchange of chemical systems – a method known as Molecular Communication (MC). In the example herein outlined, communication is achieved by exploiting the fluorescence properties of quantum dots which are then analysed in the domain of the Fourier transform. The problem is studied from a theoretical point of view by numerically solving the differential equation that governs the phenomenon. The theoretical results are exploited to develop a prototype MC platform that enables the communication of infection-induced temperature variations via biological fluids to a receiver that has the task of releasing an antibiotic drug.

ce:section.ce:para: Communication is a key driver of human advancement. The ability to collect and store information enables us to more effectively share and spread knowledge. Similarly, technological progression requires fast and secure information exchange between people and machines. The last century has witnessed numerous communication breakthroughs as the efficiency and security of both wired and wireless approaches have reached new levels of advancement. However, ubiquitous wired communication remains unrealistic due to infrastructure constraints, especially in very large or in very small environments. Noise is a universal limiting factors for the performance of wireless communication [1]. The origins of electromagnetic noise can be thermal background noise, ground noise, and radiated electromagnetic signal from electrical and electronic systems. Electromagnetic noise and radiated electromagnetic interference can overlay the transferred signal or can interfere with electronic devices [2] and in such a way reduce the signal-to-noise ratio [3]. Because of these reasons, scientific interest toward novel communication media has increased in recent years. The focus is on media which can be successfully employed in environments where “conventional” approaches fail. There are two fields which stand to gain immediately and significantly from this research. The first is cybernetics and cyber-medicine and the implementation of communication networks within biological systems would finally merge these fields. One of the major aims within reach is real time monitoring of key biological parameters such as blood pressure and body temperature. This would transform treatment for those suffering from diseases requiring continuous therapies or continuous monitoring. Technological advances in this direction include wearable devices able to provide information on parameters such as physical activity, heart rate, and sleep patterns [4–6]. However, these devices have serious limitations due to their limited number of sensors which cannot locally monitor an organ or a specific part of the body. For example, patients who undergo hip implantation surgery are prone to inflammation in the vicinity of the operation, which, if left untreated, can lead to serious and acute inflammation, requiring additional surgery [7,8]. Localised temperature monitoring could assess the possible effects of hyperthermia around the prosthesis, enabling timely pharmacological treatment in the early stage of such inflammation, eliminating the need for additional surgery. The other key problem is that existing message transferring Micro-devices [9,10] induce permanent radiative transfer with biological tissue.

The second field which stands to gain from advances in MC involves undersea communication. Electromagnetic waves travel differently under water – they cannot travel the distances achieved on land. Although this drawback may be partially overcome by the construction of fixed place communication exchange infrastructure, when immediate communication of position is crucial for rescue operations, such as for submarines, ships, or airplanes it remains an imperfect form of communication. Innovators are inspired to overcome these types of problems by developing new methods of communication in which information exchange between devices avoids electrochemical radiation. This is the case with MC. It is a bio-inspired approach and consists in encoding nanoparticles (NPs), acting as information vectors [11]. Storing information within nanoscale information particles or writing data with molecules are bio-inspired methods because they are processes seen extensively in the natural world [12–14]. For example, molecular messengers are dispersed/released inside blood for the communication between organs in animals. In plants, sap is used for communication trough xylem and phloem and air for the dispersal/release of hormones [15,16]. Indeed, the MC observed in nature is highly targeted because it is based on accurate, efficient, and specific coupling between messengers and receptors, which is very hard to reproduce. Thus, state of art working prototypes for MC applications are currently mostly oriented towards non-specific communication using basic signal modulation methods for the encoding and decoding of information. Typical modulation methods are essentially based on information particle type (MoSK, molecular shift keying) and/or concentration (CSK, concentration shift keying) [17]. The most common is called OOK (On-Off keying) [18]. Based on CSK modulation, it is a method in which information is transferred with binary code. If the time window is fixed between subsequent symbols, the logic can be described as follows:
Eq.1
C
( t )
=
{
>
C threshold
f o r
b i t
1
<
C threshold
f o r
b i t
0

Where C(t) is the concentration at time t and C
threshold an arbitrary threshold. This modulation strategy requires the release of several bits to transfer even very simple information. Hence, the overriding problem: information exchange within the communication channel is too slow because it is limited by the drift velocity of the transport medium (for example, blood or sap). For communication platforms aimed at rapidly communicating the variations of physiological parameters (for example, temperature) the speed of information transfer needs to be much faster.

When MC information is transmitted in binary code, a single letter using, for example the extended ASCII coding, must be transferred using 8 bits (8 time slots in OOK modulation). To avoid intersymbol interference (ISI), the time slot needs to be large enough relative to the dispersion of the NP information pulse [19]. Thus, fast transferring of complex messages such as temperature values or other concentrations is rendered useless by the time needed to complete the message. Increasing the speed of the molecular messenger carrier seems to provide a solution in some cases. However, the principal application field of MC is implanted biodevices in which the flow speed of the carrier (for example, blood) is unmodifiable. One solution could be to increase the quantity of information encoded/stored within a single release of messenger, avoiding the need for multiple bit release. To write more symbols in the time slot, the information could be written with several C
threshold values, achieving the modulation method called n-CSK (n-ary Concentration Shift Keying). However, in n-CSK the error associated with the ISI strongly depends on the number of thresholds chosen, and therefore on the number of bits per symbol [20]. Alternatively, if the information written in each time slot depends on the type of molecule, the modulation method is called n-MoSK (n-ary Molecule Shift Keying) [17].

The high information density MC has been theorized but proven implementation is yet to be reported. To be achieved, the choice of molecular messenger is critical to accomplish efficient transmission between transmitter and receiver. The modulation scheme requires a receiver capable of detecting the correct structure of the molecules – like a mass spectrometer [21]. However, it is an invasive and time demanding technique. For in vivo MC applications, and for emergency communications, two things are essential: a non-invasive receiver and rapid detection of the molecular messenger. The presence of ultra-compact optical fibers [22] facilitates these requirements as they enable exploitation of the absorbance/fluorescence optical properties for the detection of molecular messengers in biological fluids. Other desirable properties for molecular messengers are that they are: easily synthesized, non-toxic, biocompatible, soluble/dispersible in aqueous fluids, and easy to detect. It was shown that carbon-based fluorescent NPs are excellent candidates [23–25]. Since their discovery in 2004 [26], carbon fluorescent NPs have been widely studied and used in various fields of chemistry, biology and materials science. In fact, they are often used as fluorescent markers for bioimaging, biosensing, chemical sensing, optoelectronics, photonics, and solar cells [27–29]. Based on their internal carbon structure, they are divided into Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) and Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) [30]. Their fluorescence properties depend strongly on their structure. It is possible to modulate the emission frequency as well as the fluorescence yield, depending on the size, shape and structure. Also, alteration of the graphene core with heteroatoms is a known method to modify the fluorescence properties [31,32]. It is precisely this property that we have herein exploited to realize a high-density information communication platform.

We thus propose herein the use of GQDs as suitable information NPs to realize an efficient n-MoSK modulation MC by exploiting specific fluorescence. Of note, the first working prototypal high information density MC platform exploiting the molecular communication approach to transmit temperature variation was achieved (using architecture like that seen in Fig. 1 ) The transmitter encodes temperature values to be transmitted by a.) three discrete Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) and.) b mixtures of them with three discrete fluorescence spectra, referred to as B-GQDs (Blue), C-GQDs (Cyan) and G-GQDs (Green). They travel through the transport channels toward the receiver which decodes them.

The Blue, Cyan and Green GQDs were purchased (from Aldrich) and used as received. They were then diluted in ultra-pure water obtained by filtration (from Millipore). The fluorescence properties were analysed using a Cary fluorometer (from Agilent) using a 5 nm slit. The prototype platform was manufactured in-house as schematized in Fig. 2 .

The control centre consisted of a standard PC interfaced to a stm32-based microcontroller. A customized Python script was developed for data acquisition. The microfluidic system consisted of the following: a piston pump controlled by a stepper motor to ensure constant flow rate and a Teflon tube connecting the transmitter and the receiver. The piston pump was designed in-house and made from a combination of purchased components (from RS Components) and in-house 3-D printed parts (using Alfawise u30 pro). The detection of the fluorescence emitted by the nanomessengers was performed in flow by means of a modified HPLC detector (from Shimadzu) which was capable of scanning the emission. Simulations were performed by numerically solving the equation with a Python script using the NumPy and FiPy libraries.

In a typical advection-diffusion regime MC system, a transmitter releases chemical messenger pulses inside the communication channel. It is transported by the advective flow and dispersed according to laws of fluid dynamics. The variation of concentration over time in each area of the transport channel is described by Eq. (2) of advection-diffusion (ADE).
Eq.2a
∂ C / ∂ t =
∇ ·
(
D ∇ C
)
−
∇ ·
(
ν C
)
+ S

Where C (mol/L) is the chemical messenger concentration, t time (s), D the coefficient for the dispersion (mm2/s) contribution from stochastic diffusion and turbulences caused by flow in the channel, v the flow velocity (m/s) field extrapolated from Navier-Stokes equations, and S is the source or the sink (mol/L). The simulation is performed in a domain that is relative to a pipe with zero slip velocity walls. Carrier entry is allowed through one of the two openings with a constant flow rate. The velocity vector is parallel to the axis of rotational symmetry of the pipe. The other opening of the pipe is set as an outflow with no overpressure or suction whatsoever. The source of the molecular messengers is defined as the initial condition, i.e., when t = 0. No sink is allowed except for spillage from one of the tube ends.
Eq.2b
∂ P / ∂ t =
∇ ·
(
D ∇ P
)
−
∇ ·
(
ν P
)
+ S

In particular, the probability distribution P(t) of the messenger is calculated by solving equation (2b) numerically [33,34]. [33,34] We setted the size of the communication channel (200 mm length and 0.35 mm radius), the velocity (0.4 mm/s), the diffusion coefficient (1.2 mm 2/s) and the initial pulse width (5 mm), the probability distribution of observing the molecular messenger at the receiver over time is shown in Fig. 3 a. A short pulse representing bit 1 becomes a wide and distorted Gaussian-shaped signal. In a real-world MC apparatus, the probability would be altered in relation to messenger concentration as detection in relation to function of time one or more of physicochemical properties of the messenger increases. We reported the measure of fluorescence as a function of time as congenial [19,23–25,35]. The fluorescence intensity is a function of the concentration according to Eq. (3), where I represent the fluorescence intensity, k a proportional parameter, A absorbance, and E fluorescence yield - which increases with concentration and decreases upon entry of the self-quenching regime [36,37].
Eq. 3
I
( P )
= kA
( P )
E
( P )

Assuming that the concentration is kept adequately low, the following is true: I(P) ∝ C. If the excitation wavelength and the detection wavelength are kept constant a linear relationship can be assumed and the fluorescence intensity at the receiver, for a single signal, will have the same shape as in Fig. 3a. A high information density MC system requires the release of several molecular messengers. A single wavelength does not guarantee the discrimination of messengers. However, a system able to acquire the entire fluorescence spectrum would enable complete information content recording during a single time slot via release of several messengers. The 3D plot shown in Fig. 3c represents what the receiver would ‘see’ if it could acquire the entire fluorescence spectrum for each dt interval. This plot is obtained by considering an entire fluorescence spectrum (a Gaussian curve is simulated) reported in Fig. 3b) and the same P(t) in Fig. 3a. Although it could be possible to achieve an instantaneous multiwavelength detection of fluorescence with complex optical instrumentation, the microscale in operando application herein proposed is based on cyclical scanning of the wavelength's range – considered more achievable in a miniaturized implanted device. The red line traced on the surface in Fig. 3c represents the back-and-forth cyclical wavelength scans performed according to a cos(ωt) function. On the plane P(t) vs Time the red line projection, obtained by several scans representing the receiver-detected signal, is shown. It is easily demonstrable that the shape of the detected signal will depend on several parameters: i) the profile of the P(t) obtained by solving the advection-diffusion equation, ii) the fluorescence spectrum under consideration iii) the frequency with which the scanner oscillates iv) the phase between the wavelengths scanner and the releasing time of the messenger from the transmitter and v) the carrier flow speed of the channel. We have extensively explored all these parameters to optimize the design of the communication system. For the sake of brevity, we report the most significant results. Fig. 4 a shows the signals obtained from the simultaneous release of two information NPs with distinct fluorescence spectra (different Gaussian shapes). Both signals, including the Fourier transforms (see Fig. 3b), are markedly different enabling us to state with confidence that the proposed strategy can be used to create a working high information density MC system.

Fig. 4c shows the signals detected by the receiver when a single fluorescent information particle is released at different times. Unexpectedly, in this case, signals are not superimposable although they are related to the same substance. This is due to the mismatch between the arrival time of the messenger and the position of the wavelength scanning detector, making it difficult to recognize the messenger if the transmitter and the wavelength scanner are not perfectly synchronized. A similar effect occurs when the speed of the transporting medium varies, which is very common in the case of biological fluids. However, signals are superimposable in Fourier transform space (Fig. 4d), suggesting that it is better to analyse the signal of the Fourier transformation in order to avoid such problems. Indeed, the approximation of messenger fluorescence spectra by Gaussian curve is unrepresentative of reality because real fluorescence spectra are not free of noise, not symmetrical, and they often have “shoulders” and “tails”. Therefore, hybrid simulations were carried out rather than the simulated fluorescence spectra and the experimental spectra of the three GQDs with the respective binary and ternary mixtures obtained at 290 nm excitation wavelength is shown in Fig. 5 .

By releasing 3 different messengers (including mixtures) 23 discrete symbols (including no signal) can be transferred per single time slot according to Eq. (4).
Eq. 4
Symbol
content =
{
i f
C
( B − GQDs )
> k
f i r s t
b i t
i s
1 ,
t h e n
0
i f
C
( C − GQDs )
> k
s e c o n d
b i t
i s
1 ,
t h e n
0
i f
C
( G − GQDs )
> k
t h i r d
b i t
i s
1 ,
t h e n
0
Where C
x is the concentration of a specific molecular messenger and k is the selected threshold to consider as bit 1 reaches the receiver.

Fig. 6 .a reports the signal detected by the scanning receiver when 4 discrete symbols are transmitted. Clearly, the more complex the message, the more challenging identification and recognition. Additionally, as already mentioned, the difficulties related to the non-perfect synchronization between transmitter and detector result in discrete signal shapes at the receiver, even if the same symbol is transmitted. Real-world applications of MC demand fast and unambiguous recognition of the released symbol and whenever a pulse travelling through biological fluids reaches the receiver, it must be instantly recognised and decoded. Thus, a discrimination strategy is required. The most appropriate solution is to employ machine learning to create a learning set enabling instruction at the receiver according to a given dataset of symbols. Thus, a set of 35 simulations related to 7 symbols with 5 different arrival times with a delay of 33 s separating them, was built. Then, each signal obtained was transformed to the Fourier space. For the construction of an efficient dataset for these types of signals, we considered the first four relative maxima of the Fourier transform, excluding 0 frequency. A 4 × 35 matrix is obtained from the simulated signals, relative to the three GQDs and their respective mixtures and arrival times at the receiver. In order to simplify the study of the correlation between all the 35 signals (i.e., objects), described in the matrix of 4 relative maxima (i.e., variables), we applied a multivariate analysis for principal components (PCA) [38] to reduce the number of variables. This type of analysis enables the creation of a new set of perpendicular axes (i.e., Principal Components) in the directions of maximum dispersion of the matrix points, in the n-dimensional space. The first principal component (PC1) follows the direction of maximum dispersion of the points, the second principal component (PC2) follows the direction of maximum dispersion of the points perpendicularly to the direction of the first component, and so on. A simple interpretation of the data, specifically in relation to our problem, consists in the analysis of the Scores-Plot obtained from this transformation of the variables. Objects that are close in the Scores-Plot are seen by the model as alike.

The Scores-Plot reported in Fig. 7 indicates that with this approach, 7 dense clusters are obtained. Thus, the classification quality is very high and, as is evident from Fig. 7, it is impossible to observe the dispersion of the points inside each cluster because of their proximity to each other. The inset shows a zoom-in of the B_C-GQDs cluster. It indicates that the blue spots in Fig. 7 are in fact clusters of points. What this confirms is that although the signal profile is relatively convoluted, use of Fourier transformation of the signal makes identification of the symbol simple – by means of only 2 PCs – and the non-synchronization problem is considered negligible. As a test set of this decoding method, we underwent an extensive experimental campaign by means of a prototypal MC platform, reproducing at the laboratory scale a similar situation to the one we simulated (see materials and methods for details). In Fig. 8 a we report the signal obtained by release of Blue, Green, Cyan and the mixtures Blue-Green and Blue-Cyan-Green Graphene Quantum Dots respectively, representing several specific sequences of 1- or 0-bits. As expected, all the signals have a predictable shape due to the unique fluorescent spectrum of the substance or mixture that composes it. Following the same approach described previously, we extrapolated the first four relative maxima of the normalized Fourier transform, excluding that of 0 frequency, and we fitted the experimental dataset with the model related to the learning dataset (Fig. 7).

Fig. 8b shows the PCA Score-Plot fitting the experimental data as test dataset. The predictivity of the method is proficient for mixtures of two or more GQDs because the fluorescence spectra cover a large portion of the analysed region. Therefore, the scope for errors in the interpretation of information is less. Small errors due to imperfect synchronization of the fluorescence wavelengths scanner are more evident with narrower fluorescence spectra and with B-GCDs there is an especially significant difference between PC2 and the learning plot.

With the use of a more sophisticated artificial intelligence predictive method, we expect more precise symbol recognition. However, this form of machine learning optimization is beyond the scope of our research project. Nevertheless, although still not tested in vivo, these encouraging results have strongly motivated us to apply this innovative method of communication to operational conditions in real-world situations. The high information density MC we developed is highly applicable to the field of information transfer via implanted physiological sensors and actuators [19]. In these fields, important physiological parameters would benefit from sensory monitoring, control, and possible intervention via drug administration, including, for example, in monitoring of tissue temperature and the possible onset of bacterial inflammation. To give a specific example, HMEC-1 cells (Human Dermal Microvascular Endothelial Cell Line) show temperature variation in the presence of LPS (lipopolysaccharides) of the bacterial shell. The measured ΔT [39] variations are of the magnitude of about 0.2°, 0.4°, 0.8° and 1.2° relative to increasing concentrations of LPS.

The MC system we are presenting herein is positioned exactly for this type of application. A temperature sensor could constantly communicate significant temperature variations, corresponding to the presence of LPS interacting with the cell, via release of fluorescent information NPs within the biological fluid (the communication channel).

A receiver located in another area of the body would decode the information sent by the temperature sensor triggering release of an antibiotic as/when required. In this context, the speed and efficiency of information transmission is of fundamental importance. An OOK modulation method based on binary encoding is not applicable as it would be far too slow and would require a high number of symbols to transfer complex information. Fig. 9 a shows just how many bits are required to send information this way and how long it takes to transfer a single ΔT value encoded in ASCII code. Conversely, the high information density MC proposed by us involves the use of three GQDs, including mixtures, enabling the transfer of up to 8 discrete symbols by single release, enabling an octal-coded communication system. Considering the amount of information the temperature sensor must transfer, it is convenient to use coding made specifically for the system. It is also useful to use some of the symbols available for checking-status functions as confirmation that the system is working. Table 1 reveals the logic proposed for this problem. As seen, information is assigned to a pulse of fluorescent information NPs. The choice is not random but dictated by the PCA results obtained previously. The B_C_G-GQDs are those that show a minor reading error, so they were chosen for the most frequent type of information, namely check-status.

The mixtures C_G-GQDs and B_G-GQDs contain the start information of the reading – positive and negative temperature change, respectively. The others contain the information of the absolute value of the temperature variation – in well-defined steps. Fig. 9b demonstrates how to send a temperature value following the specific coding described in Table 1, revealing an experimental example of transmission of ΔT = "+0.8". The signals in blue, obtained through the release of B_C_G-GQDs, are the periodic "check" signals necessary to check the correct functioning status of the communication channel. The time window between 1500 and 3000 s contains all the information to be sent. The first signal, obtained by releasing B_G-GQDs, enables the receiver to read a positive temperature variation. The absolute value of the variation, 0.8°, is finally encoded in the signal obtained by the release of G-GQDs. By keeping all the experimental parameters constant, approximately 25,000 s (7 h) are needed to transmit information as complex as a temperature value OOK modulation in binary code (see Fig. 9a). Under these conditions, more complex modulation methods are required, and more symbols are available, allowing the development of ad hoc codecs for the system and considerably reducing the message transmission time. In fact, we demonstrated that the time needed to transmit the same message is reduced to about 1500s (from the 1500s–3000s as seen in Fig. 9b) if several symbols and the coding described in Table 1 are used. The time saved enables other symbols to be used for error checking or other operational functions. Fig. 9b (signal at 500s and signal at 4250s) provides an example of the check information transmission. Communication efficiency can definitely be improved in terms of bandwidth and transmission speed; however any optimization is beyond the scope of this paper.

We have demonstrated that the information density transported by molecular messengers can be significantly increased by simultaneously employing and detecting messengers with discrete physico-chemical properties. Specifically, three carbon-based NPs, characterized by three discrete fluorescence spectra, were employed to transmit three bits information in a single chemical pulse. Additionally, we have proven that, by analysing the signal Fourier transform rather than the signal itself and by employing a simple machine learning approach, it is possible to overcome any interference/artifacts caused by non-perfect synchronization between source and detector and to train a model for the recognition of the message contained in high density information NPs. Our approach is easily scalable by altering particle information or detection techniques thus demonstrating, in our opinion, that the realization and application of effective in vivo MC systems is drawing significantly closer.

Luca Fichera: Methodology, Software, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Giovanni Li-Destri: Validation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Nunzio Tuccitto: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors thank “Programma ricerca di ateneo UNICT 2020-22 linea 2” Proj. NatI4Smart for funding. Authors thank Prof. Giovanni Marletta for his valuable advice, stimulating discussions, and mentoring.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Paulraj, A.; Nabar, R.; Gore, D. Introduction to Space-Time Wireless Communications. In Introduction to Space-Time Wireless Communications; Cambridge University Press, 2003; pp 1-270.
